title: Good Morning America Full Broadcast - Tuesday, March 3, 2026
author: ABC News
fullText: Good morning, America. For our viewers in the west, a US embassy struck. Americans urged to leave the Middle East. The war with Iran intensifies. Breaking overnight. Air strikes ramp up. The American death toll rises. We projected four to 5 weeks, but uh we have capability to go far longer than that. >> More American forces now heading to the region. The White House warns the hardest hits yet to come. The next phase will be even more punishing on Iran than it is right now. >> After nearly 2,000 strikes on Iran across the first 48 hours. This morning, this nuclear facility damaged. And new video of the dangerous mission. An American aviator on the ground in Kuwait after three F-15 jets were shot down by friendly fire. >> You're safe. You're safe. Everything good? No problem. Thank you for helping us. >> Urgent evacuation. Americans in the region told to get out now with Iran firing at at least 11 countries. The US embassy in Saudi Arabia struck by attack drones, airports, hotels hit. The Americans unable to leave. This morning, mixed messages from the White House about its reasons for starting the war. >> We knew that there was going to be an Israeli action. The Secretary of State now saying the attacks were preemptive. And with Iran saying the strait of Hermuse is closed, oil prices spiking, what it means. Our team is live across the region and Washington. Bill and Hillary Clinton's deposition tapes released. >> If you guys are doing that, I am done. >> The former president answering questions about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein. >> I did take those plane trips with him and you have a right to ask those questions. And what happened during the former Secretary of State's deposition? >> Oh, for heaven. >> So, I would like to >> emergency landing. >> Come this way. >> Hundreds of passengers forced to evacuate, sliding down a United plane with smoke pouring out of an engine. What happened on board? Snow and ice heading east. But with a big warm-up finally on the horizon, Ginger's must-see demonstration. >> What if I were to fall in? How do you survive? Oh yes, absolutely. Uh shock. >> What to do if you fall through ice total eclipse >> in the totally incredible view outside our windows early this morning, captivating millions. Live from ABC News headquarters in New York City, it's Tuesday. Good Morning America. Good morning, America. We want to get right to the breaking news in the war with Iran. The rest of the region is getting pulled into the conflict. And overnight, there was an urgent warning from the State Department for all Americans in at least six countries to leave the Middle East. Now, >> the US is now sending more troops to the region as Secretary of State Rubio warns Iran that quote, "The hardest hits are yet to come and the next phase will be even more punishing." Uh so we'll have the latest on how the next phase will play out as a US death toll rises. We have team coverage this morning. Chief foreign correspondent Ian Panel starts us off. He's there in Tel Aviv for us. Good morning Ian. >> Yeah, good morning Robin. From a region reeling from a war that's already escalating. We're just hearing that the allies have bombed a meeting taking place to elect a new Supreme Leader inside Iran amid fears the war could last longer than predicted. Overnight, explosions rocking Tehran and sites across the Middle East. This morning, the Natan's nuclear facility damaged in a strike. But the UN nuclear watchdog saying there's no radiological consequence. It comes as the American death tolls rising. The Pentagon now confirming six troops killed in an Iranian strike on a tactical operation center in Kuwait. At least 18 more service members seriously wounded across the region. As the White House warns, the hardest hits are yet to come. >> The next phase will be even more punishing on Iran than it is right now. >> This as the US reveals even more American forces will be heading to the region. This rapid buildup of forces demonstrated the joint forces ability to adapt and project power at the time and place of our nation's choosing. President Trump even saying he's not ruling out US forces on the ground and admitting it could be a prolonged conflict. Whatever the time is, it's okay. Whatever it takes, we will always and we have from right from the be beginning, we projected four to five weeks, but uh we have capability to go far longer than that. >> In the first 48 hours of the operation, the US carried out nearly 2,000 strikes on Iran across at least 50 locations. Del bombers dropped 2,000lb bombs on underground facilities. Fighter jets destroyed. Multiple Iranian Navy ships sunk. Iran's Revolutionary Guard headquarters leveled. The major focus, Iran's missile launchers and those deadly one-way attack drones, which are now wreaking havoc across the Gulf. Overnight, Central Command releasing new video showing US strikes on Iranian ballistic missile launchers. But analysts say Iran still has thousands of missiles in its arsenal. The dangers of the mission also coming into clearer view. >> You're fine. >> Really? >> New video showing an American aviator on the ground in Kuwait having safely ejected from her F-15 jet. One of three shot down by friendly fire Monday. All six crew members safely ejecting as the aircraft spiral to the ground. >> You safe. You're unsafe. Everything good? No problem. Thank you for helping us. >> In Iran, the death toll now at least 780 according to the Iranian Red Crescent. Huge search and rescue efforts are underway across the country after USIsraeli strikes targeted 130 cities. Overnight, the IDF announcing they're expanding operations, opening a second front in Lebanon, deploying troops to the southern part of the country, and striking Iranian proxy Hezbollah. ABC's Matt Rivers is in Beirut. >> So, you can hear that's the latest Israeli air strike. And another, you can see the smoke coming there behind me. That's one of dozens of air strikes that the Israeli Air Force has been carrying out, not only here in Beirut against Hezbollah targets, but across this entire country and what has really become a second front in this ongoing war. As the war spreads throughout the Middle East, it appears all sides are preparing for a long haul. the head of Iran's National Security Council writing on X, "We'll fiercely defend ourselves and our 6,000 years old civilization regardless of the costs and will make the enemies sorry for their miscalculation amid questions about how long this uh war is going to last." President Trump posting overnight that US stockpiles of munitions have never been better, while admitting that reserves of higher grade munitions isn't where he'd want it to be. But interestingly, he also wrote that wars can be fought, quotes, forever and very successfully using just these supplies. Remember, this is a president who said he'd end forever wars. Michael, >> yeah, we won't forget that, Ian. Thank you for that. And the state the State Department is now urging Americans to leave the region as Iran escalates their attack. Chief international correspondent Jane Longman is in northern Iraq. And James, options are very limited for Americans to leave right now. >> Yeah, absolutely, Michael. Well, Iran is unleashing hell on this region with more American targets in the firing line by the hour. Israel and the US are trying to take out Iranian military capabilities and yet still drones and missiles are raining down. And now two US embassies in the Gulf have been forced to close. Chaos across the Middle East this morning as more of this region is pulled into war. The US embassy in Saudi Arabia struck by two Iranian drones hitting overnight. No injuries reported, but US officials warn of an ongoing threat with at least eight other drones intercepted in surrounding cities. And an urgent warning this morning from the State Department, telling all Americans in the Middle East, from Egypt to Saudi Arabia and Israel, to depart now via commercial transportation, issuing evacuation orders for non-emergency personnel and family members in at least six countries. But getting out is very hard. The US ambassador to Israel warning Americans there that their options to leave are very limited and there are few good options, suggesting they shuttle a bus to Egypt in order to get a flight back to the US. More than 70 drones and missiles soaring across Iraq. And in Kata, multiple missiles seen being intercepted in the skies above Doha. At least 11 countries, including the European nation of Cyprus, now caught in the crossfire. In Dubai, where missiles hit a luxury hotel and rocked the world's largest airport, filling a terminal with smoke, flights are now starting to resume. The Iranians are mimicking what the Russians are trying to do to Ukraine, and that is to use these drones to create terror. They're hitting civilian targets, hotels, airports, places where civilians are that have nothing to do with any military objectives. >> I'm anxious, I'm scared, and I'm desperate to get home. Americans like Shakina Lee from Chicago, who was there celebrating her boyfriend's birthday, are still struggling to get out. >> We're not getting any support from our airline. We've been trying to get in contact with them, but due to the volume of the situation, they're not able to field calls. So, none of our questions are getting answered. >> She says they had to stay overnight in the parking garage of her hotel. >> Garage was packed with families from all over the world just desperate to know what's going on. >> And take a look at this. Flight radar shows the disruption all across the region. And you can see how complicated it's getting for people stuck here to get home. Now, an Iranian tactic with all these strikes is to cause friction with important US allies. And we're already seeing Trump falling out with the UK prime minister who said he won't carry out regime change from the skies. George. >> Okay, James. Thanks. Let's bring in retired General Doug Loot who is Ambassador NATO and a White House adviser on Iraq and Afghanistan for both Republican and Democratic presidents. General, thank you for joining us this morning. We've seen both the president and the secretary of state warned that bigger strikes are coming. What are the next steps likely to look like? >> Well, I think what they were referring to, George, is this calculus, what you might call the military math of this conflict. And on both sides, you have target sets, so what you want to find and what you want to hit. You have the ability to hit those targets, so offensive systems. And then you have on the other side of the equation, defensive systems. And I think when the US signals that it's going to up the ante, it intends to send additional strike systems and additional in particular additional defensive systems because we've seen that the Iranians uh retain an ability to hit relatively soft targets like the embassies that you've reported. Right. One of the big questions going forward is who's going to run on munitions and defenses first. >> Right. I mean, this is this is very much a contest uh of attrition between those the ability to attack and the ability to defend. Right now, the offensive side of that equation on both sides, both our ability to strike Iran and Iran's ability to strike back have has dominated this equation. Uh we'll see over time whether that offensive dominance persists. >> Well, finally, the president has notably not ruled out putting American boots on the ground. Will they be necessary? Well, this uh this has to do with the sticky problem, the unanswered question of the connection between the air campaign and a political end state. What we've learned in the past is that in order to actually control the situation sufficiently to determine a political end has required presence on the ground and if we rule that out, then we're in a very uncertain period of trying to dictate results from the air alone. >> General Doug Loot, thanks very much. Robin >> and George, the Trump administration, as we know, has been offering various justifications for the campaign against Iran. Chief White House correspondent Mayor Bruce is tracking what the president and others in his administration have been saying about the conflict. Good morning to you, Mary. >> Good morning, Robin. Well, President Trump is adamant that the US will quote easily prevail in this war, but his administration is sending mixed messages about why the US decided to launch this campaign and what comes next. The Secretary of State now suggesting that Israel forced the US's hand. This morning, a new explanation from the White House about why the US started a war with Iran. The Secretary of State saying the US attacks were preemptive, that Israel was going to strike Iran with or without the US. >> We knew that there was going to be an Israeli action. We knew that that would precipitate an attack against American forces. and we knew that if we didn't preemptively go after them before they launched those attacks, we would suffer higher casualties. >> The president has insisted Iran posed an imminent threat to the US. >> The regime already had missiles capable of hitting Europe and our bases, both local and overseas, and would soon have had missiles capable of reaching our beautiful America. >> But American intelligence agencies say Iran would not have had missiles capable of reaching the US for another 9 years. The president predicts the US will quote easily prevail in this war and is dismissing concerns that he will tire of this fight if it drags on beyond the four to five weeks that he's projected. >> Somebody said today they said, "Oh well, president wants to do it really quickly after that. He'll get bored. I don't get bored." >> Moments later though, Trump distracted by the White House decor. >> I picked those drapes in my first term. I always like gold. The president isn't ruling out sending in US troops if necessary, telling the New York Post, "I don't have the yips with respect to boots on the ground." Like every president says there will be no boots on the ground. I don't say it. As for the future of Iran, the administration with more mixed messages, the president has been urging the Iranian people to overthrow the government. But at the Pentagon, Secretary Hegth adamant regime change was not the US objective. This is not a so-called regime change war, but the regime sure did change and the world is better off for it. >> Some of Trump's longtime supporters in the MAGA movement raising serious concerns. >> It's pretty clear uh that we're not going to gain anything more. And how do we know that? Because no one has explained what we're there to gain. >> On Capitol Hill, the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee questioning the president's justification for starting this war now. If we equate a threat to Israel as the equivalent of an imminent threat to the United States, then we are in uncharted territory. >> Now, the administration is set to brief the full Congress later today. Overnight, the president sent formal notification of these strikes to the Hill, but Democrats are demanding that this war be put to a vote, and the House is expected to take up a war powers resolution later this week that would block any further US military action in Iran without congressional approval. But George, that is unlikely to become law. >> Okay, Mary Bruce, thanks very much. Primary season for the midterm election kicks off this morning in the shadow of the war. Chief Washington correspondent John Cer is here with a look at some of the key contests. And John, Texas front and center. >> Uh, absolutely. Look, the midterms are here. Primaries today. We have Arkansas, North Carolina, and Texas. But Texas is the big one because Democrats believe this is the year they can win in a state that has not elected a Democratic senator in nearly four decades. The Democratic Senate primary fits pits Jasmine Crockett against state legislature uh legislator and aspiring Presbyterian minister James Talerico. I was just in Texas, George, and I can tell you that both of these candidates have been electrifying Democrats, but they have starkly different visions of what the Democratic Party should be doing. With Jasmine Crockett, you have somebody who is known for her aggressive takedowns of Trump's cabinet secretaries. She says the goal is not to win over Republicans, but to energize Democrats as a fighter going all out in resistance to everything Donald Trump. James Terico, on the other hand, he hardly mentions Trump at all on the campaign trail. He is a progressive, but he is aggressively trying to win over independents and Republicans with a rejection of what he calls the politics of division. He's been attracting huge crowds and not just in Democratic cities, but also in Republican parts of the state. George in his speeches, you will hear him quote the Bible, but you will also hear him quote Bad Bunny. >> But whether either one of the those candidates can win will really depend on what happens in this Republican primary. >> Yeah. And I got to tell you, on the Republican side, we are witnessing one of the nastiest Senate primaries that I have ever seen. John Cornin is attempting to win a fifth term in the Senate. And Cornin and his allies have spent approximately $70 million. This is presidential primary level spending, almost all of it directed against Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton, who Cornin is calling a cheat, a liar, and a fraud, and warning that if he wins, Republicans will lose in Texas. >> We'll be watching it today, John. Thanks very much. And coming up, the video released from Bill and Hillary Clinton's depositions in the House's Jeffrey Epstein investigation. We're going to tell you what we're learning about their testimony. >> Also, ahead, Sam had the air scare. Michael, it's a United Airlines flight cross country making an emergency landing because of a smoking engine. Now, that forces passengers to evacuate. We will hear from some of those passengers this morning. >> That was really a scary moment, Sam. Okay. And will, you have the latest on the search for Nancy Guthrie. >> Yeah, good morning, Robin. Savannah Guthrie and her family making their first visit to the tribute area set up outside her mom's house. They're sharing a new message. That and other headlines new at 7:30. >> We'll be right back on this Tuesday morning. Stay right there. Hi, I'm Joan London from Fair Oaks, California, and this is my daughter Jamie. Good morning, America. >> That is a classic moment. Our dear friend Joan London on her first day as co-host in 1980 along with her 7w week old daughter Jamie. Well, now this morning, we're excited. Joan is going to be back with us telling us all about her new memoir, taking us behind the scenes of some of her favorite moments in the studio. We cannot wait to catch up with Yeah, that is going to be fun. First, let's go to Will with the latest headlines. >> Good morning, George. Lots to get to this morning at 7:30. Beginning with overnight, a third victim from the attack at the bar in Austin, Texas, died, identified as 30-year-old Jorge Peterson. The other two killed in Sunday's rampage where 21-year-old Savvitha Shawn and 19-year-old Ryder Harrington. Multiple others were injured in the shooting, which police are investigating as a possible act of terrorism. and Savannah Guthrie and her family made their first visit to the tribute area set up to their mom, Nancy Guthrie, outside her house yesterday. Nancy Guthrie has now been missing for over a month. The Today Show host followed up the visit with an Instagram post talking asking people to keep praying and hoping. And overnight, a small plane making an emergency landing on ice on the Hudson River. It was a single engine plane hitting the river near Newberg, about 50 mi north of New York City. Both passengers on board were able to escape and swim to shore. New York Governor Kathy Hokll saying the passengers sustained only minor injuries. And an incredible show in the skies this morning, a blood moon total lunar eclipse. That's when the Earth moves directly between the sun and the moon that gives the moon a deep red look. Hopefully you caught it because there won't be another total lunar eclipse until New Year's Eve of 2829. And I'm sorry that I didn't advertise this yesterday so we all could have looked out for it. You showed it to us. It's on me. But there you go. It's on GMA this morning. >> Thank you. Well, we're going to turn now to the videos of last week's depositions of Bill and Hillary Clinton in the House's investigation into Jeffrey Epstein. Chief investigative correspondent Aaron Kurski here with the details. Good morning, Aaron. >> Good morning to you, George. This was the first time a former president was compelled to testify before Congress. Bill and Hillary Clinton wanted public depositions. The committee kept them private, but we can now see President Clinton say he saw nothing to make him think Jeffrey Epstein was a sex trafficker. This morning, the extraordinary image of former President Bill Clinton deposed. >> I did take those plane trips with him. And you have a right to ask those questions. >> Answering questions about his relationship with the late sex offender Jeffrey Epste with whom Clinton was friendly in the early 2000s before Epstein was charged with sex crimes. There's nothing that I saw when I was around him that made me realize he was trafficking women. >> Clinton, who was accused of no wrongdoing, was also questioned about his own conduct. >> Did you ever have any sexual contact with young women or girls in the presence of Epstein or Maxwell? >> No. The former president was asked about this photo from the Epstein files showing him in a pool with Epstein's co-conspirator, Galain Maxwell, and an unknown woman. >> It appears there's a this photo there's a girl over here or there someone. >> Yeah, I don't know who that is. >> Clinton recalled a conversation with President Trump in the early 2000s when he said Trump told him how his relationship with Epstein fell apart. He said, "You know, we had some great times together over the years, but we fell out all because of a real estate deal." And he said, "I'm sorry it happened." That's all. >> Trump has denied any wrongdoing and has described his falling out with Epstein differently, accusing Epstein of stealing employees from Mara Lago. From the beginning of her deposition, Hillary Clinton repeatedly told lawmakers she never interacted with Epstein or knew about his crimes. You have compelled me to testify fully aware that I have no knowledge that would assist your investigation in order to distract from President Trump's actions and to cover them up. >> And then Clinton learned Congresswoman Lauren Boowbert leaked a photo of the closed door deposition. I'm done with this. If you guys are doing that, I am done. You can hold me in contempt from now until the cows come home. This is just typical behavior. >> We will go off. >> Oh, for heaven. >> So I would like to understand how that permissible the hearing was. >> It doesn't matter. We all are abiding by the same rules. >> I I will take that down. >> Yeah. I would like to take a break at this moment. I'd like to haveation for now. >> Secretary Clinton exasperated by the committee's behavior there, at least Representative Boowbert's. One person the committee has not called to testify about his relationship with Epstein is President Trump. Another is Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnik, who was photographed on Epstein's private island. Michael, >> maybe we'll get to that one day. All right, Aaron, thank you. Going to turn now to the passenger plane forced to make an emergency landing returning to LAX because of a possible engine fire. Well, this morning, we're hearing from some of the passengers. And Sam, you have the story. >> Just hearing you say it right now, I'm thinking about the panic, Michael. For more than 200 people on board that United flight, they had to use emergency slides and those air stairs to get off that plane fast. All while smoke was indeed pouring from an engine. >> Jump this way. Jump inside. Get away from the engine. This morning, dramatic video capturing the moment hundreds are forced to evacuate a smoke-filled New York, New Jerseybound United flight in Los Angeles. >> A few minutes after takeoff, there was s sort of this broom vroom, very, very bumpy feeling when one of the engines was on fire on the lefth hand side. The flight attendant was, you know, panicking, saying, "Oh my god, oh my god, I've never seen this before. This is really bad." >> United 2127 is You have one engine shut down. >> Affirmative. We do. United 2127. The pilot of the New Jerseybound plane telling air traffic control they received warnings of a fire in the left engine and they needed to evacuate. >> We're getting fire indications still, so we're going to evacuate tonight 2127. >> The plane diverts back to LAX for an emergency landing. >> Leave all bags. I repeat, do not bring your bag. >> I was in the emergency exit row and learned the hard way. You know, it ain't just about extra leg room. Passengers told to leave everything behind using the inflatable slides to escape to the taxi way below. Running away from the plane to safety. Smoke seen billowing from the engine and the emergency prompting a temporary ground stop at LAX. United Airlines saying flight 2127 safely returned to Los Angeles to address a possible engine fire and that no one on board was seriously injured. You know, they always ask you if you're prepared to open that emergency door, >> but are you? I mean, they had to. United says there were no injuries except amazingly for a couple of cuts. No one had to be taken to the hospital. Everything went well. >> Pay attention when they come up. Just don't dismiss it. >> That's true. All good advice there. Thank you, gentlemen. Now, in GMA, and we've seen many incidents of people falling through ice this winter, sometimes with catastrophic results. Now, as temperatures get warmer, the danger may grow as ice starts to melt. And Ginger has a mustsee demonstration of what to do if it happens. >> Yes. And we were just saying, you know, part of this country had the coldest winter in 30 years. So places that don't ice over very often did. And you have a whole generation of people that are kind of learning what to do on ice. It may look solid and safe enough to walk on, but underneath that surface, it could be much thinner than you know. So, I went to a lake in Indiana with a team of rescue divers so that I could show you what you need to know to stay safe. From falling through it to rescuing someone in it, icy water under frozen rivers and lakes can be deadly. So, we came here to Loganport, Indiana, and this huge frozen quarry. But how can you tell how thick the ice is? And what if I were to fall in? How do you survive? A lot of times people don't realize how thick or how thin the the ice is. You could get trapped underneath or could injure yourself uh hitting your head on the way down. >> One of the most obvious signs that it might not be safe is if some of the ice is already visibly thawed. Generally 4 in is the minimum you want to walk or skate on ice. So you could take a 4-in drill bit, go ahead and go down, and if it's still ice all the way down and you don't hit water, you're in good shape. Test it again. 100 ft and another 100 feet. You want to make sure cuz ice can be different at different places in the lake. Experts say if you don't know, don't go. The ice we're standing on now is about 8 in thick. So, if we want to get in, we're going to need to cut. Cue the rescue divers of the Indiana State Police. And since this is only a simulation, they cut out a clean triangle shape. That way, I don't get cut by the jagged edges of the ice when I eventually take the plunge. I'm also attached with a safety line, so if I go under, they can pull me back up. But before I go in, we're going to show you how to get someone else out. Zach, one of our rescue divers, he's going to play the victim. The first thing to do is have someone call 911. Then it's all about keeping yourself safe first. They say, "Preach, reach, throw, row, and go." Preach or tell the victim how to self-rescue. Kick your legs, get horizontal, and pull yourself along the ice. If you're safely close enough, reach the victim with something like a hockey stick, ladder, or almost everybody's got a jacket on, so you can get that as close to the victim as possible and give them a little leverage to get out. >> If you can't reach the person that fell in, find something to throw, like a rope
